The problem appeared when I tried to use the "vanilla" `/deposit/new` to create a new deposit.
These are the steps followed by the `UI`:
 1. Create the deposit with POST
 2. Read from the links the bucket endpoint
 3. Push the file to the bucket endpoint with PUT.

Without this "hack", the [bucket is created](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-files/blob/master/invenio_records_files/api.py#L291) when the deposit is serialized in JSON, i.e. after the session commit().
It means that the bucket doesn't exist anymore when the file is uploaded.

Maybe we should force the creation on [deposit creation](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-files/blob/master/invenio_records_files/api.py#L250)?
Something like:
```python
class FilesMixin(object):
   def create(self, *args, **kwargs):
      self._create_bucket()
      super(FilesMixin, self).create(*args, **kwargs)
```

When using invenio-records-files this should probably be changed to ``files`` -> ``_files``. See http://pythonhosted.org/invenio-records-files/usage.html, especially http://pythonhosted.org/invenio-records-files/usage.html#dumping-files

Also I'm a little confused about the philosophy of how this was set up.  If you are setting this up as a  way of drawing a single image that is sampled more finely than the native pixel scale, and then recovering multiple images at the native pixel scale by de-interleaving, then I would think you would have a pixel response that corresponds to a **larger** physical scale than the drawn image.  (i.e., your native pixel scale is x but you draw it into a pixel scale that is x/2, then de-interleave.)   Right now you have a 1 arcsec pixel response, then draw into an image with 2 arcsec pixels, so you're sampling *less* finely; what is the use case for that?

Here we need to careful. When I wrote above, that I was unsure if you handled the non-iterable case correctly, it was because I was unsure if the under lying call takes a single position or a list of position. I now see that it takes a list, which of course means that the non-iterable input case is correct.

However, if it does take a list, I would highly suggest to turn all iterable input args into a list and execute only one request. The reason for this is two-fold:

 1. Performance, we should of course never prematurely optimize, but I think this just makes sense. For apps doing network IO, that will always be the "slow thing" and since it is made so easy to do all at once we might as well do it.
 2. Deletions. The significantly more important issue, is that if we do only do one request, we can hope that everything will also be executed as one query on the other end and this is important for deletions. We went through this somewhere else, where the call only took one argument, maybe it was for queue manipulation. Consider this, if I ask this method to delete tracks 0 and 3. After having deleted track 0, the second track that I wanted to delete is now in position 2 **not** 3. This can be fixed by sorting deletions descending, but here, at least of it works, it would be soooo much easier to just do one request. It may also be, that they actually iterate on the other end, in which case, we have to handle this problem here.

Deletions stuff, and the example above, should be tested.

The one thing that I do not know is, if the list form accepts mixing operations, move and delete, or if it might separate them out on the other end. This will make everything more complicated.

It's a bit different in the `Ncp` set handlers. If the operation fails, we still need to report the failure status in a spinel response to host, so we should not go to exit and return this error code. The `errorCode` we return from the `set` handler is actually any error in sending the spinel response (not the error in performing the operation). 

`SetPropertyHandler_MAC_SRC_MATCH_EXTENDED_ADDRESSES()` is example code of where we do use two different variables to track the error and use `VerifyOrExit`:
-  `errorStatus` for error of the set operation (to pass in spinel response). 
- `errorCode` for the error to return from the `SetProp` method.

You are correct that the motivation for this change is to align with a broad range of stacks.  Many only allow all-at-once registration during stack init.  I imagined this call to be repeatable, which would handle your case of services across multiple files.

The main change in semantics is that services and characteristics need to be registered in linear order: `S1, C11, C12, S2, C21, C22`.  

The previous API implied that multiple services could be registered and then characteristics could be added later to any of the services in any order: `S1, S2, C11, C21, C12, C22`. This later order is no longer allowed.

The NULL termination is convenient in that there is no need to manually track and pass the lengths and provides consistent handling across both characteristics and services.  I agree that the mechanism feels a bit more hacked since the termination is a zeroed structure field rather than a proper NULL pointer.

Honestly, I would rather not merge yet. I see the value of what we are trying to do but I don't think we are there yet. I also think there is some confusion here. But that might be due to the latest changes in NAPALM or due to the lack of "common goals" for this PR.

First, some notes and clarifications regarding napalm.

> I would recommend keeping the tutorial in alongside the rest of the code, rather than as a separate repo

The repo `napalm` contains no code. It's only a placeholder for documentation and a wrapper to install all the drivers. The common code of napalm lives now in `napalm-base` and each driver has a dedicated repo, ie, `napalm-eos`, `napalm-ios`, etc... There was some discussion in the mailing list about this a month or two ago and the latest release notes has also some explanations around this and why we did it. In any case, what I meant by having a dedicated repo where the support files, not the content. However, I don't mind having the support files in the main `napalm` repo either, at the end of the day, this is one of the main reasons of this repo.

> the tutorial instructions should be tested alongside the code, to ensure that the instructions stay current. By "testing" here, I mean actual automated testing, and not relying on people to remember to test things when they change code. This isn't actually done here yet, we could backlog that idea.

That is not necessary. Two reasons:

1. The API is stable, that means that code written following napalm API should work, always, no matter what.
2. Each driver has it's own set of integration tests that are run automatically every time someone pushes new code. So we should find bugs and issues there without the need of testing the quickstart. I am not claiming the tests are perfect so feel free to comment if you think something could be improved. Bottom line here is that we already have automated testing covering this.

> "Release early, release often" is a good thing to follow, sometimes.

Agree, that was mostly the reason behind the latest refactoring. That and smaller PRs. However, features should be complete, releasing something that doesn't work might just frustrate people and make them lose confidence on the project so I am all for releasing quick and fast, as long as is complete. Specially if it's documentation. A common concern on OSS is that documentation is confusing or that you can't just follow it because you always find surprises.

> A colleague was asking for this quickstart material, if this gets into master and onto "readthedocs" it will help our team. :-)

But this is not about one particular's use case. It's about the community. Being a contributor has the advantage that you can focus on your particular problem but we should still aim to build something as generic as possible so everybody could get the benefit of it. I know that's probably not what you meant but I felt obliged to clarify anyway ; )

Back to the PR itself. Right now, I think that most of the content of this PR mostly duplicates work already done in the tutorials. So let me try to explain what I envision for this PR:

1. The target audience should be developers/network_engineers/users_in_general that heard about napalm and what to see it in action and maybe try it by themselves. The key word here is **users**. Anyone that might be considering napalm as their main interface to the network. They could be people looking into ansible, writing their own scripts or something else. But definitively not people trying to contribute to napalm. For that people we will have some other document.
2. The goal of the document should be to be able to copy & paste a few lines and see different things you can do with napalm:
   1. Change configuration
   1. Get diffs.
   1. Get LLDP neighbors/BGP/interfaces/whatever
   1. Test the CLI tool. 
3. Provide a list of resources to explore a bit further.

Right now, most of the stuff is covered on the tutorials, what is missing is:

1. How to quickly build a lab so you don't have to test on your own network.
2. Fixing the tutorials so you can actually copy & paste them.

In summary, my problem with the PR right now is that overlaps a lot with the tutorials. I would rather have this PR just telling you how to start a lab to move forward onto the rest of the tutorials and adapt the tutorials so they can be reproduced with the lab.

I hope it makes sense, otherwise, feel free to send me back a wall of text :P

It seems like there's a fundamental issue with the GoDaddy API functionality here (i.e., there's no delete operation, just a replace, which is inherently race-y), which we'll probably just have to live with.

It also seems like the GoDaddyPy library could be safer. It does a [full replace](https://github.com/eXamadeus/godaddypy/blob/master/godaddypy/client.py#L277) of all DNS records for the domain during deletion, which could cause data loss if other, potentially unrelated, DNS changes are being made while Certbot is running. Instead, it could do a [limited replace of records that match the type and name](https://developer.godaddy.com/doc#!/_v1_domains/recordReplaceTypeName), which would only impact other ACME challenges.

Better yet would be doing a limited replace _and_ checking the record content in addition to the name and type. Without that, users are likely to encounter issues if multiple instances of Certbot run concurrently.

Would you be interested in working with the library maintainer to see if these changes could be made upstream? If you'd prefer, I'd be happy to reach out to them.

There are two things that convince me this design makes sense:
1. It supports a really valuable scenario - detach management of an existing resource from Pulumi without destroying the resource.  For example, this would be useful when splitting one Pulumi stack into multiple stacks and factoring some resources out of the original stack to be `.gets`.
2. If we didn't do this special case behaviour, the operation would be guaranteed to fail - we would delete the resource immediately before (or worse, immediately after - or even worse, non-deterministically before/after) the `.get` tries to capture a handle to it.  It's hard to imagine what benefit it provides to allow this to happen, especially given that (1) is a more useful interpretation.
